_meta.keyKnowledge: We are learning to design and run simulations to estimate probabilities that cannot be calculated easily.

_source.w: We are learning to design and run simulations to estimate probabilities that cannot be calculated easily.
y: Simulations are a powerful tool for estimating probabilities in situations too complex for theoretical calculation.
Designing a simulation requires careful thinking about how to model the real situation using random processes.
Monte Carlo simulations are used professionally in finance, engineering, climate science and drug development.
eg: Designing a simulation to estimate the probability that at least 2 people in a class of 30 share a birthday.
Using a random number generator to simulate drawing marbles from a bag with and without replacement.
Running 10,000 simulations of a game of chance to estimate the probability of winning.
Simulating a quality control process: 'If 5% of items are defective, what is the probability that a batch of 10 contains at least one defective item?'
Comparing simulation estimates with theoretical calculations to verify accuracy.
Discussing how increasing the number of simulation runs improves the reliability of the estimate.
